entities.summary: British Warburg Institute historian; Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition (1964) established the 'Yates thesis' making Hermetism central to Renaissance intellectual history; foundational figure whose work generated the scholarly field this portal documents, even as the thesis has been substantially revised

entities.name: Francis Mercury van Helmont
entities.summary: Flemish philosopher and Christian Kabbalist; Kabbala Denudata (1677–84) was the major Latin compendium of Jewish Kabbalah; bridged Renaissance Hermetic philosophy and early Enlightenment via Leibniz and Anne Conway

entities.name: Frank Klaassen
entities.summary: Historian of medieval and Renaissance magic; Transformations of Magic (2001) traces how the learned ritual magic tradition (Ars Notoria, angelic manuals) transformed through manuscript transmission and print; bridges the medieval and Renaissance magic traditions that the Yates framework leaves underexamined

entities.name: Giordano Bruno
entities.summary: Italian Dominican friar turned philosopher; executed by the Roman Inquisition (1600) for heretical cosmological and theological doctrines; his infinite-universe cosmology drew on Copernicus and Nicholas of Cusa but was embedded in a Hermetic-magical framework; transformed Lullian combinatory art into a Hermetic memory system for impressing the animated cosmos on the practitioner's mind; Frances Yates's Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition (1964) made him the central figure of her Hermetic thesis

entities.name: Giovan Battista Della Porta
entities.summary: Neapolitan natural philosopher; author of Magia Naturalis; founder of Accademia dei Segreti; defined natural magic as peak of natural philosophy operating through occult properties, without demonic mediation

entities.name: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola
entities.summary: Italian philosopher; Oration on the Dignity of Man (1486) is the Renaissance's most eloquent statement of human freedom and perfectibility; his 900 Conclusiones (1486) — condemned by Innocent VIII — included the first systematic Christian Kabbalistic theses, arguing that Kabbalah confirms Christian doctrine; synthesized Hermetism, Neoplatonism, Kabbalah, Aristotle, and Arabic philosophy; settled in Florence under Medici protection, in direct intellectual exchange with Ficino

entities.name: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entities.summary: German polymath and occult philosopher; author of De Occulta Philosophia, the foundational Renaissance synthesis of natural, celestial, and ceremonial magic

entities.name: Hermes Trismegistus
entities.summary: Legendary author of the Corpus Hermeticum; syncretic figure combining Greek Hermes and Egyptian Thoth, imagined as a contemporary of Moses transmitting the same prisca theologia; Isaac Casaubon's 1614 philological demonstration that the texts are early centuries CE compositions permanently changed their scholarly reception; they remain philosophically significant as evidence of Neoplatonist culture regardless of their fraudulent origin claim, and as the primary source for Ficino's Hermetic synthesis

entities.name: Iamblichus
entities.summary: Syrian Neoplatonist (c.245–c.325); De Mysteriis Aegyptiorum (c.300) provided the most systematic ancient philosophical defense of theurgy — arguing that divinely instituted ritual operations, not philosophical contemplation alone, prepare the soul for union with the divine; his insistence that theurgical power derives from the gods' own nature rather than human convention underwrites Renaissance ceremonial magic's philosophical self-defense; the dominant Neoplatonist for the Florentine Academy's engagement with ritual

entities.name: Isaac Casaubon
entities.summary: French-Swiss classical philologist; De Rebus Sacris (1614) dated the Corpus Hermeticum to early centuries CE through linguistic analysis, refuting the prisca theologia claim of ancient Egyptian origin; his philological demonstration permanently changed how the Hermetic texts must be read, arriving after a century and a half of influence

entities.name: Isaac Newton
entities.summary: English mathematician and natural philosopher; Principia Mathematica (1687) established mathematical mechanics as the foundation of modern natural philosophy; his private alchemical program — over one million words of alchemical writing — demonstrates that the 'Scientific Revolution' did not simply expel the Hermetic tradition but incorporated and transformed it

entities.name: Jacob Boehme
entities.summary: German Christian mystic, shoemaker, and theologian; Aurora (1612) presented a spontaneous theosophical vision of the divine ground, the generation of the divine persons, and the spiritual structure of nature — drawing on Paracelsian chemical imagery, Kabbalistic symbolism, and Lutheran piety; Boehme's influence on German Romanticism, Schelling, and Hegel makes him a key figure in the transmission of Renaissance Hermetic-alchemical themes into later Western intellectual history

entities.name: Jean Bodin
entities.summary: French jurist and political philosopher; De la Démonomanie des Sorciers (1580) was the most influential demonological treatise of the late sixteenth century, systematically refuting Weyer's skepticism from a Neoplatonist framework and providing a handbook for witch-trial practice; Reginald Scot's Discoverie of Witchcraft directly responds to Bodin

entities.name: Johann Valentin Andreae
entities.summary: German Lutheran theologian; attributed author of the Chemical Wedding of Christian Rosencreutz (1616), the most literary Rosicrucian manifesto — an alchemical allegory combining spiritual transformation, emblematic imagery, and court entertainment; Andreae later called it a youthful Ludibrium, though the Rosicrucian movement he helped generate was culturally significant

entities.name: Johann Weyer
entities.summary: German physician and student of Agrippa; De Praestigiis Daemonum (1563) argued accused witches were victims of diabolical delusion and melancholy; foundational text in the medical critique of witch-trial doctrine

entities.name: Johannes Kepler
entities.summary: German astronomer; discoverer of planetary motion laws; his 1619 polemic against Robert Fludd crystallizes the methodological rupture between the Hermetic-qualitative tradition and emerging mathematical-quantitative natural science — the divergence that produced the modern distinction between 'science' and 'occultism'

entities.name: Johannes Reuchlin
entities.summary: German humanist and Hebraist; De Verbo Mirifico (1494) and De Arte Cabalistica (1517) are the foundational texts of Christian Kabbalah — arguments that Kabbalistic interpretation of Hebrew reveals Christianity's truth encoded in the Old Testament; his defense of Jewish books against Pfefferkorn's destruction campaign (1509–1514) galvanized the humanist movement and established the legitimacy of Hebrew learning for Christian scholars

entities.name: Johannes Trithemius
entities.summary: German Benedictine abbot and humanist scholar; Steganographia composed a systematic angel-magic communication system while embedding genuine cryptographic ciphers within the angelic framework; Polygraphia is the earliest printed work on cryptography; teacher and correspondent of Agrippa, who drew on Trithemius's treatment of celestial spirits for De Occulta Philosophia

entities.name: John Dee
entities.summary: English mathematician, astronomer, astrologer, and occult philosopher; advisor to Elizabeth I on navigation and calendar reform; the angelic conversations conducted with Edward Kelley (1581–1589) produced the Enochian language and celestial system — the most elaborate angelological system of the Renaissance; Monas Hieroglyphica (1564) presents a mathematical-symbolic account of the unity of the cosmos through a single hieroglyphic symbol; held the largest private library in Elizabethan England

entities.name: Keith Thomas
entities.summary: British social historian; Religion and the Decline of Magic (1971) is the most comprehensive study of magical belief in English popular culture — sociological rather than intellectual-historical in approach; argues magic declined through social and economic change rather than scientific refutation; complements Yates and Walker with attention to practice over philosophy

entities.name: Liana Saif
entities.summary: Historian of Arabic-Islamic and European intellectual traditions; The Arabic Influences on Early Modern Occult Philosophy (2015) is the primary reference for the Arabic-to-Latin transmission thread in RenMagDB's genealogy

entities.name: Marin Mersenne
entities.summary: French Franciscan friar and mathematician; his attacks on Robert Fludd in Quaestiones celeberrimae in Genesim (1623) combined theological concerns about Hermetism with methodological critique of analogical reasoning; together with Kepler's polemic, represents the institutional pressure that marginalized the Hermetic tradition in the seventeenth century

entities.name: Marsilio Ficino
entities.summary: Italian philosopher, Catholic priest, and translator; head of the Florentine Platonic Academy under Cosimo de' Medici; his Latin translation of the Corpus Hermeticum (1463) and Plato (completed 1484) gave the Latin West its first full access to these traditions; Theologia Platonica (1482) synthesized Neoplatonism and Christianity; De Vita Coelitus Comparanda (1489) is the foundational philosophical account of natural talismanic magic; Walker's distinction between spiritual and demonic magic was built on close reading of Ficino's strategies for legitimizing his practices

entities.name: Moses de León
entities.summary: Spanish Kabbalistic author; principal composer of the Zohar (Book of Splendor), the foundational text of Jewish mysticism, cast as teachings of Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai; the Zohar's theosophy — ten sefirot, cosmic exile, divine union — became the primary source through which Pico, Reuchlin, and Agrippa accessed Jewish Kabbalah for Christian Kabbalistic synthesis

entities.name: Nicholas of Cusa
entities.summary: German cardinal and philosopher; De Docta Ignorantia (1440) argued that the infinite God exceeds rational comprehension and can be approached only through 'learned ignorance' — acknowledgment of the limits of human knowing; his cosmological corollary that the infinite universe has no fixed center directly influenced Bruno's infinite cosmology; his mathematical mysticism (using mathematical paradoxes to approach divine infinity) influenced Dee's symbolic mathematics in the Monas Hieroglyphica

entities.name: Paola Zambelli
entities.summary: Italian historian of Renaissance thought; White Magic, Black Magic and collected essays document the Arabic-to-Latin transmission of astrology and magic; scholar of Agrippa, Pomponazzi, and the political dimensions of magical persecution

entities.name: Paracelsus
entities.summary: Swiss physician and alchemist; tria prima doctrine (salt, sulphur, mercury) replaced Galenic humoralism with chemical medicine; theosophical cosmology of signatures and elemental spirits shaped Fludd, Dee, and the broader Paracelsian tradition

entities.name: Plato
entities.summary: Athenian philosopher; founder of the Academy; dialogues on the soul, Forms, cosmology, and the One formed the bedrock of the Neoplatonic tradition that Renaissance magic drew on via Ficino, Plotinus, and Proclus

entities.name: Plotinus
entities.summary: Egyptian-born Neoplatonist philosopher teaching in Rome; the Enneads (arranged by Porphyry) articulate the system of divine emanation — One, Intellect, Soul, Matter — that grounds all subsequent Neoplatonism; the doctrine of world-soul pervading and animating the cosmos provided Ficino's philosophical basis for natural magic; his account of the soul's ascent through philosophical contemplation structures the spiritual dimension of Renaissance magical theory

entities.name: Porphyry
entities.summary: Neoplatonist philosopher; disciple and biographer of Plotinus; his edition of the Enneads (c.301) preserved Plotinus's work for posterity; the Isagoge (Introduction to Aristotle's Categories) became the standard medieval logic textbook; his Letter to Anebo challenging the philosophical basis of theurgy provoked Iamblichus's De Mysteriis as a systematic defense — the central ancient debate about whether ritual practice or philosophical contemplation alone can unite the soul with the divine

entities.name: Proclus
entities.summary: Late-antique Neoplatonist; head of Platonic Academy in Athens; Elements of Theology (c.450) axiomatized Neoplatonic metaphysics into 211 propositions on the Euclidean model — hierarchical divine emanation from the One through divine henads, Intellect, and Soul; his works on theurgy provided the philosophical framework for ritual operation that Ficino, Agrippa, and Dee draw on; Liber de Causis, a compilation from his Elements, circulated attributed to Aristotle throughout the medieval West

entities.name: Ramon Llull
entities.summary: Catalan philosopher and missionary; Ars Magna established combinatory art generating all propositions from divine attributes; transformed by Bruno into a Hermetic memory system and by Leibniz into universal language theory

entities.name: Reginald Scot
entities.summary: English author of The Discoverie of Witchcraft (1584); argued both accused witches and demons lacked the powers attributed to them; Protestant polemic against witch persecution and Catholic ritual magic

entities.name: René Descartes
entities.summary: French philosopher; Discourse on Method (1637) and subsequent works articulated the mechanistic dualism — res cogitans vs res extensa — that eliminated the spiritus, world-soul, and celestial sympathy on which Renaissance natural magic depends; produced 'the death of nature' (Carolyn Merchant's phrase) that made Ficino's and Agrippa's conceptual foundations philosophically untenable

entities.name: Robert Fludd
entities.summary: English Paracelsian physician and Rosicrucian philosopher; Utriusque Cosmi Historia (1617–21) is the most elaborately illustrated cosmological synthesis of the Renaissance, integrating Hermetic, Kabbalistic, alchemical, and musical symbolism in macrocosm-microcosm diagrams; his polemics with Kepler and Mersenne crystallize the methodological rupture between Hermetic qualitative analogy and emerging mathematical-quantitative natural science

entities.name: Roger Bacon
entities.summary: English Franciscan natural philosopher; Opus Majus argued for mathematics and experimental science; influential for theories of celestial influence; legendary magical reputation as maker of automata shaped popular image of the philosopher-magician

entities.name: Thomas Aquinas
entities.summary: Dominican scholastic theologian; Summa Theologiae established the canonical categories (natural occult properties, licit spiritual invocation, illicit demonic pact) that Renaissance magical practitioners had to navigate

entities.name: Vittoria Perrone Compagni
entities.summary: Italian philologist and Renaissance scholar at University of Florence; critical edition of Agrippa's De Occulta Philosophia; specialist in the sources and textual transmission of Renaissance occult philosophy

entities.name: Wouter Hanegraaff
entities.summary: Dutch historian at University of Amsterdam; Dictionary of Gnosis and Western Esotericism (2005) institutionalized the field; Esotericism and the Academy (2012) provides the third-wave methodological framework treating 'Western esotericism' as a scholarly construct requiring historical analysis rather than uncritical acceptance — the approach underpinning this portal

Anyone who had the good fortune to know Charles Schmitt, to study with him, or to read his many books and articles will know how much better this history would have been had so learned and creative a scholar lived to finish it. The present volume (not counting notes and bibliography) runs to about 117,000 words. Charles left a draft of about 40,000 words, of which perhaps a fifth dropped out in rewriting.

entities.name: "Astrologi hallucinati." Stars and the End of the World in Luther's Time. by Paola Zambelli
entities.summary: Clulee reviews Zambelli's edited volume Astrologi hallucinati, which examines the cultural and intellectual impact of the 1524 planetary conjunction prediction, exploring intersections among astrological prognostication, Reformation-era religious controversy, and political propaganda.
entities.sourceRef: "Astrologi hallucinati." Stars and the End of the World in Luther's Time. by Paola Zambelli — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: (Draft)
entities.summary: Edward John Langford Garstin, though a somewhat distant cousin, was familiar to me by the name of Eddie from my early childhood. I don’t recall in what connection I first heard of him but his name seemed to come up in any talk of family relations. I did not meet him until 1928 when I was studying art at the SIade School and had joined The Quest Society. This group was run on fairly informal lines by G.R.S. Mead, one time secretary to Mme Blavatsky, and author of many works on Gnosticism.

entities.name: (Variorum Collected Studies 1063) Allen, MichaelJ. B - Studies in the Platonism of Marsilio Ficino and Giovanni Pico-Routledge (2017)
entities.summary: Acknowledgements “The Renaissance: Platonism,” The Columbia History of Western Philosophy, ed. Richard H. Popkin (New York: Columbia University Press, 1999), pp. 303–315 “Cultura hominis: Giovanni Pico, Marsilio Ficino and the Idea of Man,” Giovanni Pico della Mirandola, Convegno internazionale di studi nel cinquecentesimo anniversario della morte (1494–1994), Mirandola, 4–8 ottobre 1994, ed. Gian Carlo Garfagnini (Florence: Olschki, 1997), pp.
entities.sourceRef: (Variorum Collected Studies 1063) Allen, MichaelJ. B - Studies in the Platonism of Marsilio Ficino and Giovanni Pico-Rou… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: 00_Frag_FM.indd
entities.summary: The projected image of the philosopher Pico della Mirandola and the painter Raphael share a common trait: both were seen by contemporaries as angels. The role Pico and Raphael themselves played in the establishment of this assimilation is discussed, as is the theological and intellectual background of the function of angels in this period.

entities.name: 13570FM.pgs
entities.summary: Findlen edits a multi-author volume on Athanasius Kircher (1602-1680), the Jesuit polymath, examining his activities in Rome, his engagement with Coptic and Kabbalistic scholarship, his paleontological and magnetic theories, and his role as a node in the Republic of Letters, with contributions addressing Kircher's relationship to figures including Bruno, Peiresc, and the Society of Jesus.

entities.name: 16775 Article Text 16440 1 10 20170821 saif arabic influences occult phil
entities.summary: An anonymous reviewer assesses Liana Saif's The Arabic Influences on Early Modern Occult Philosophy (2015), which traces how Islamic Neoplatonic and Aristotelian synthesis shaped Western occult doctrines from the twelfth century onward. The review highlights Saif's argument that Arabic astrological and magical theories, grounded in microcosm-macrocosm correspondences and celestial causation, formed an integral part of the Western philosophical tradition rather than a marginal curiosity.
entities.sourceRef: 16775 Article Text 16440 1 10 20170821 saif arabic influences occult phil — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.summary: Norrgrén analyzes John Dee's marginalia in his copy of Pantheus's Voarchadumia (1530), arguing that most annotations compare the text with Pantheus's earlier Ars Metallicae rather than expressing Dee's own interests. Norrgrén contends that the non-comparative marginalia, probably written in 1559, demonstrate Dee's early interest in cabbalistic methods for gaining knowledge about natural substances, suggesting greater continuity in Dee's intellectual development than previously assumed.

Norrgren examines John Dee's marginalia in his copy of Pantheus's Voarchadumia (1530), arguing that the annotations, probably written in 1559, reveal Dee's early interest in cabalistic methods for gaining knowledge about natural substances and provide evidence of greater continuity in Dee's intellectual development than previously assumed, with the hieroglyphic monad symbol appearing abundantly five years before publication of the Monas Hieroglyphica.

entities.name: 9782503550640_txt.pdf
entities.summary: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola (1463–1494) focuses on Anaxagoras (ca. 500–428 BC) because he considers him as a precursor of the the later Neoplatonic concept all things exist in all things in their own mode, which became the core of Pico’s metaphysics. Anaxagoras’s philosophy permits Pico to establish his doctrine that all things share a portion of God within them, in their own way. Pico rejects the fixed position of man in the ontological hierarchy. Man has the chance to become everything.

entities.summary: The purpose of this book is not to show that one of the most prominent fig- ures in the philosophy of the fifteenth century, Giovanni Pico della Mirandola (1463–1494), was a ‘scholastic philosopher’, at least as this term has been com- monly understood.1 He most definitely was not. Nor was he a ‘Platonist’ or a ‘Kabbalist’, these being the preferred yet highly suggestive appellations which are frequently deployed by modern scholars to characterize the scope and point of his work.

entities.name: Alphabet of Nature
entities.summary: Coudert and Corse translate and annotate Franciscus Mercurius van Helmont's Alphabeti vere Naturalis Hebraici brevissima delineatio, which argues that the Hebrew alphabet is the natural language reflecting the physical movements of speech. Published in the Aries Book Series on Western Esotericism, the edition situates van Helmont's linguistic Kabbalism within seventeenth-century Christian Hebraism.

entities.name: An Introduction to Jacob Boehme
entities.summary: Hessayon and Apetrei edit an interdisciplinary collection bringing together leading authorities on Jacob Boehme to trace his thought and four centuries of reception. This does not mean that the one is independent of the other, that the form of Ramon Llull’s message did not deeply in� uence its matter, or, more importantly, that the matter and, above all, its goals, were not primary in determining its form. We will, in fact, frequently touch on such subjects, but only as they come up in connection with the form.

A search in Google with his name raises nearly a mil­ lion results, he is also the subject of a large number of books and articles. Kircher was interested in a great diversity of subjects about which he published a large number of books, so that he has been called “the last man who knew everything.

entities.name: Fabula 2018 nov 07 vol 59 iss 3 4 McDonald William C Trithemius and the Legend of the Wild Hunt 10 1515 fabula 2018 0100 libgen li
entities.summary: It is well-established that Johannes Trithemius (1462–1516), the renowned polymath (theologian, cryptographer, and chronicler), entertained an avid interest in all things concerning magic. Scholars have neglected, however, to pay heed to his views about The Wild Hunt/Wild Huntsman, a widely propagated tapestry of legends rooted in popular mythology. We examine four passages from his chronicles to shed light on the many facets of this lore.
entities.sourceRef: Fabula 2018 nov 07 vol 59 iss 3 4 McDonald William C Trithemius and the Legend of the Wild Hunt 10 1515 fabula 2018 0100… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.summary: Edelheit traces the evolution of humanist theology through Ficino, Pico, and Savonarola from 1461 to 1498, examining how each figure attempted to reconcile philosophical and theological concerns. Published in Brill's Medieval Mediterranean series, the monograph focuses on the development of a distinctively Florentine form of Christian humanism grounded in Neoplatonic and Scholastic sources.

entities.summary: Zambelli surveys the career of Carlo Ginzburg from The Cheese and the Worms through his work on Piero della Francesca, situating his microhistorical method within broader debates about historical narrative. Zambelli highlights Ginzburg's attention to the relationship between literary technique and historiographical practice, tracing his intellectual debts to figures ranging from Manzoni to Lucien Febvre.

entities.name: From Sinai to Athens: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola’s philological quest for the transmission of theological truth
entities.summary: In the summer of 1486, Pico came into possession of what he regarded as the original Chaldean text of the Chaldean Oracles, whose Greek text thus came to appear to him as an incomplete and flawed translation. The now-lost purported original Chaldean Oracles were a back translation infused with kabbalistic elements produced by Flavius Mithridates. In his Conclusiones nongentae (Rome, 1486), however, Pico devoted to them fifteen conclusions.
entities.sourceRef: From Sinai to Athens: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola’s philological quest for the transmission of theological truth — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Gantenbein Urs Leo 4 Converging Magical Legends Faustus Paracelsus and Trithemius Introduction to an Old Association libgen li
entities.summary: Gantenbein traces the converging legendary traditions associating the historical Faust, Paracelsus, and Trithemius, demonstrating that all three were traveling scholars with interests in magic and the mantic arts whose biographical similarities led to increasingly intertwined reputations from the sixteenth century onward, culminating in Goethe's use of Paracelsian material for his Faust figure.

entities.name: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola and Astrology (1486–1493): From Scientia Naturalis to the Disputationes adversus astrologiam
entities.summary: Akopyan traces the evolution of Giovanni Pico della Mirandola's astrological views from 1486 to 1493, showing how Pico moved from early fascination with Kabbalah and Neoplatonic sources for interpreting astrology toward the comprehensive critique of the Disputationes adversus astrologiam divinatricem. Akopyan argues that the development of Pico's views on the relationship between light and matter was central to his broader project of reconciling Plato and Aristotle.

entities.name: Giovanni Pico Della Mirandola: A Study in the History of Renaissance Ideas

Cassirer emphasizes that Pico's intellectual ancestry spans ancient and medieval traditions from divergent sources, making him simultaneously representative of and outside his epoch.

entities.name: John Dee - De Heptarchia Mystica.doc
entities.summary: Peterson presents a transcription of Dee's De Heptarchia Mystica from the original manuscript (Sloane 3191), a grimoire-format compilation of instructions for communicating with angels and employing their aid, extracted from Dee's records of mystical exercises with Edward Kelley beginning in 1582.
entities.sourceRef: John Dee - De Heptarchia Mystica.doc — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: John Dee - Mysteriorum Liber Secundus
entities.summary: This is a transcription of the Mysteriorum Liber Secundus, the second book of Dee's angelic diaries from 1582, recording sessions in which the archangel Michael delivers instructions for the construction of the great seal (Sigillum Dei) and administers oaths of secrecy, while angelic figures communicate through tables of letters and numbers.
entities.sourceRef: John Dee - Mysteriorum Liber Secundus — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: John Dee - Rosicrucian Secrets
entities.summary: Edward John Langford Garstin, though a somewhat distant cousin, was familiar to me by the name of Eddie from my early childhood. I don’t recall in what connection I first heard of him but his name seemed to come up in any talk of family relations. I did not meet him until 1928 when I was studying art at the SIade School and had joined The Quest Society. This group was run on fairly informal lines by G.R.S. Mead, one time secretary to Mme Blavatsky, and author of many works on Gnosticism.
entities.sourceRef: John Dee - Rosicrucian Secrets — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: John Dee - Sigillum Dei
entities.summary: This is Clay Holden's transcription of the Sigillum Dei Aemeth, the complex seal used in John Dee's angelic magic, recreated and corrected from Sloane MS 3188. The document presents the arrangement of letters, numbers, and divine names that comprise the seal's concentric rings and heptagonal structure.
entities.sourceRef: John Dee - Sigillum Dei — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: John Dee - The Hieroglyphic Monad-Weiser Books (2000)
entities.summary: This is the Weiser Books edition (2000) of Dee's Monas Hieroglyphica in the Hamilton-Jones English translation, presenting Dee's 1564 alchemical-cabbalistic treatise on the hieroglyphic monad symbol, which synthesizes alchemy, astrology, and cabbala into a unified symbolic system. The edition includes a biographical note on Dee and a commentary on the twenty-four theorems.
entities.sourceRef: John Dee - The Hieroglyphic Monad-Weiser Books (2000) — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: John Dee The Magic Tables In The Book Of Soyga
entities.summary: Reeds investigates the magic tables in the Book of Soyga, a manuscript treasured by John Dee, reconstructing the combinatorial algorithm underlying the tables' construction and placing the work within the context of Dee's angelic conversations and his quest for an angelic interpreter of its contents.
entities.sourceRef: John Dee The Magic Tables In The Book Of Soyga — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: John Dee The Practice Of Enochian Evocation
entities.summary: This modern compilation reconstructs the practice of Enochian evocation from Dee's surviving diaries, Sloane MSS 3191, and the broader Renaissance magical literature available to Dee and Kelley. The text describes the roles of Magus and Scryer, the requisite furniture and vestments, and argues that Dee likely conducted Enochian rituals but recorded them in a diary that has not survived.
entities.sourceRef: John Dee The Practice Of Enochian Evocation — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: John Dee True and Faithful Relation (excerpts)

The edition includes manuscript provenance information and references to related manuscripts in the British Library, Bodleian, and Sloane collections.

entities.name: John Dee: True and Faithful Relation (excerpts)

entities.name: John Dee: True and Faithful Relation (excerpts)

The text exemplifies the dramatic, dialogic character of Dee's angelic scrying sessions.

entities.name: John Dee: True and Faithful Relation, (excerpts)

entities.name: kl-2014-0001d 151..192
entities.summary: Calvet reviews Sylvain Matton's critical edition of De Arte Chimica, an anonymous alchemical treatise first published in 1572 and traditionally attributed to Ficino, which Matton reattributes to Agrippa von Nettesheim. The review discusses the treatise's interweaving of Catholic theology, Greco-Roman mythology, and alchemical theory as characteristic of Renaissance alchemy.

The letters document Pico's family politics, his move to study at Ferrara, and his scholarly correspondence.

entities.name: Magic and Memory in Giordano Bruno
entities.summary: This book discusses the magical and mnemonic writings of Giordano Bruno, a 16th-century philosopher who was burnt at the stake. When I have presented my research to laymen summarized thusly, it has often been welcomed with great enthusiasm. Not only do magic and mnemonics excite general interest, but his auto-da-fé on the Campo dei Fiori in Rome seems to make the whole enterprise even more alluring. In March 2016 a colleague and I guided a group of seventeen-year-old stu- dents through Rome.

entities.name: Magic, Witchcraft, and Ghosts in the Enlightenment
entities.summary: Lynn edits a collected volume arguing for the centrality of magical practices and beliefs throughout the long eighteenth century, challenging narratives of abrupt disenchantment after the witch trials declined. The essays span Great Britain, France, Germany, and Hungary, covering astrology, alchemy, witchcraft, cunning folk, ghosts, and treasure hunters from the late seventeenth to the early nineteenth century.
entities.sourceRef: Magic, Witchcraft, and Ghosts in the Enlightenment — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Marc van der Poel Cornelius Agrippa the Humanist Theologian and His Declamations Brill
entities.summary: AGRIPPA'S LEGACY Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa (1486-1535) is both an historical fig- ure and a myth. To the modern reader, he is first and foremost the archetype of the Renaissance man, the pre-Enlightenment physician, the superstitious astrologer and black magician who tries to over- come the bonds of earthly existence. As such, Agrippa occupies his own small but significant place in European literature, as both a horrifying and an alluring figure.
entities.sourceRef: Marc van der Poel Cornelius Agrippa the Humanist Theologian and His Declamations Brill — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Marsilio Ficino (Angela Voss) NoOCR
entities.summary: “A selection of writings by the fifteenth-century philosopher and magus Marsilio Ficino, on the subject of astrology and natural magic. The editor’s introduc­ tion provides a substantial historical and philosophical context for this figure and explains Ficino’s astrology in relation to his Christian Platonic convictions"—Provided by publisher. Includes bibliographical references and index. ISBN-13: 978-1-55643-560-7 (trade paper) ISBN-10: 1-55643-560-6 (trade paper)

entities.name: Melancthon's Reply to G. Pico Della Mirandola
entities.summary: Breen presents and analyzes Melancthon's 1558 Reply to Pico della Mirandola's famous 1485 letter to Ermolao Barbaro on the relationship between rhetoric and philosophy. Breen argues that Melancthon subordinated philosophical wisdom to rhetoric and the practical life, placing the ideal of the orator above the contemplator in the Isocratean rather than the Platonic tradition.

The extracted text consists of the volume's detailed index, listing figures from Abraham Abulafia and Aristotle to Pope Alexander VI, reflecting the breadth of Pico's syncretic philosophical program.

entities.name: Microsoft Word - Liber Mysteriorum.doc
entities.summary: This is a transcription of Dee's Mysteriorum Liber Quintus (March 1583), recording angelic sessions in which Kelley and Dee receive communications regarding a book and scroll found in Northwick Hill. The text documents encounters with demonic and angelic spirits, including prayers, Latin invocations, and instructions for angelic tables.
entities.sourceRef: Microsoft Word - Liber Mysteriorum.doc — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Microsoft Word - quartus.doc
entities.summary: This is a transcription of the Quartus Liber Mysteriorum (1582), the fourth book of John Dee's angelic diaries, recording scrying sessions with Edward Kelley in which angelic figures including King Carmara and the seven Heptarchical Princes deliver instructions and visions concerning the mysteries of creation.

entities.name: Moulton_ASMAR.indb
entities.summary: xi the difference between sinful lust and divine love was so difficult that authorities believed the subtlety of the distinction was beyond the comprehension of the laity. As observers of twenty-first-century scandals surrounding clerical sexual abuse of children can attest, preaching celibacy is not necessarily an effective way to curtail sexual activity.

entities.name: Mysteriorum
entities.summary: Holden transcribes the opening of Dee's Mysteriorum Libri, prefaced by Elias Ashmole's 1672 account of how the manuscripts passed from Dee's estate through a cedar chest, a confectioner named Jones, and the warder Thomas Wale before reaching Ashmole. The provenance narrative documents the remarkable survival of Dee's angelic diaries through seventeenth-century antiquarian networks.

entities.name: Mysteriorum Liber Primus
entities.summary: Holden edits the first book of Dee's Mysteriorum Libri, recording angelic conferences beginning 22 December 1581, with Elias Ashmole's preface detailing the remarkable provenance of the manuscripts through a cedar chest discovered by a London confectioner and ultimately exchanged with Ashmole in 1672.

entities.name: Mysteriorum Liber Primus
entities.summary: Holden edits the first book of Dee's Mysteriorum Libri, a duplicate copy of the angelic conference records beginning December 1581, with Ashmole's preface recounting the manuscript provenance through the cedar chest discovered in London. This appears to be a second copy of the same text as document 149 in the corpus.

entities.name: Mysteriorum Liber Secundus
entities.summary: Holden transcribes Dee's Mysteriorum Liber Secundus, in which the archangel Michael instructs Dee in the construction of the Sigillum Dei, requiring an oath of secrecy and delivering a sequence of divine names and geometrical instructions. The text records Dee's scrying sessions with Edward Kelley, including Michael's warnings against idolatry and temptation.

entities.name: Mysteriorum Liber Tertius
entities.summary: This is Clay Holden's transcription of Dee's Mysteriorum Liber Tertius (1582), recording angel communications received through scryer Edward Kelley (E.T./E.K.), including instructions for the construction of a lamine and ring, and the revelation of angelic tables and names. The text documents Dee's systematic recording of angelic visions, including the daughters of goodness (El, Me, Ese, Iana, Akele, Azdobn, Stimcul).

entities.name: Mysteriorum Liber Tertius
entities.summary: This is a transcription of the Mysteriorum Liber Tertius, Dee's third book of angelic diaries from April 1582, recording sessions in which the angels Ese and Iana deliver tables of letters and numbers while Michael provides instructions regarding the construction of ritual implements including the lamin.

entities.name: Ninth-Century Figural Poetry and Medieval Easter Tables—Possible Inspirations for the Square Tables of Trithemius and Vigenère?
entities.summary: While it is not possible to identify exact sources for the square tables that Johannes Trithemius and, after him, Blaise de Vigenere presented in their cryptographic publications, there is good evidence that Trithemius may have been influenced by a variety of materials: He knew the figural poetry of Rabanus Maurus (780–856) that frequently used squares with a grid of 36 letters; he was fully aware of the medieval ars combinatoria and the works of the Mallorcan philosopher and theologian Raymundu...

entities.name: Noel L. Brann - Trithemius and Magical Theology
entities.summary: Brann presents a comprehensive study of Trithemius's magical theology as a chapter in the controversy over occult studies in early modern Europe, published in SUNY's Western Esoteric Traditions series. The monograph examines how the Benedictine abbot attempted to ground magical practice in theological legitimacy while distinguishing it from demonic sorcery.
entities.sourceRef: Noel L. Brann - Trithemius and Magical Theology — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Paola Zambelli White Magic Black Magic in the European Renaissance
entities.summary: Zambelli traces the shifting boundary between white and black magic in European Renaissance thought, examining how figures from Pico and Ficino through Trithemius, Agrippa, and Bruno defined, defended, and contested the legitimacy of natural magic against charges of demonic involvement. The monograph addresses continuities in the definition of natural magic, scholastic and humanist views of Hermetism, and the role of pseudepigraphy and prophecy in Trithemius's manuscripts.

entities.summary: This article argues that Ghazali’s The Alchemy of Happiness (1096–1106), The Niche of Lights (1096–1106), and The Just Balance (1096–1106)63 are the earliest works both to invoke the chain-of-being doctrine and to assert that humans, using their free will, have the capacity to transcend their station in the chain during this life. Scholarly research in this area has been scanty. European Medievalists alongside Islamic Medievalists make little mention of Ghazali’s chain-of-being doctrine.

entities.summary: Girdner argues that al-Ghazali's Book of Love from the Revival of the Religious Sciences influenced Pico della Mirandola through the mediation of Johanan Alemanno and Hebrew translations. Girdner identifies shared conceptions of a twofold human felicity combining intellectual actualization and mystical religious experience, and a common treatment of philosophical dilemmas in traditional understandings of divine love.

entities.name: Pico della Mirandola's Encounter with Jewish Mysticism
entities.summary: C HAIM WIRSZUBSKI achieved his eminence among scholars by virtue of varied accomplishments. He was steeped in the classics, he was an interpreter of Jewish mysticism, a scholar in the fields of Medieval and Renaissance Hebraism, and a distinguished teacher. He himself, however, would probably have wished to be primarily remem- bered for the contribution he made to the study of Jewish mysticism, a subject to which his commitment was profound.
entities.sourceRef: Pico della Mirandola's Encounter with Jewish Mysticism — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.summary: Lesley reviews Dougherty's edited volume Pico della Mirandola: New Essays, noting that Pico's citation of Hermetic, magical, kabbalistic, and Zoroastrian writings from Greek, Hebrew, Aramaic, and Arabic has frustrated scholarly efforts to locate his opinions within any single school of thought, and assessing the volume's contributions to this ongoing interpretive challenge.

entities.name: Pico Giovanni ERSTER TEIL Erkenntnistheorie und christliche Kabbalah Das Paradigma des Pico della Mirandola libgen li
entities.summary: This German-language study examines Pico della Mirandola as the paradigmatic figure for Christian Kabbalah, tracing his synthesis of Aristotelian, Platonic, Christian, and pagan thought and his hierarchical epistemology linking philosophy, theology, and kabbalistic illumination. The chapter argues that Pico's Commento and Oratio establish a model of the soul's ascent through successive cognitive stages that later figures, particularly Ricci, would adopt.
entities.sourceRef: Pico Giovanni ERSTER TEIL Erkenntnistheorie und christliche Kabbalah Das Paradigma des Pico della Mirandola libgen li — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Pico Giovanni Giovanni Pico della Mirandola und die Entdeckung der jüdischen Mystik im italienischen Quattrocento GIULIO BUSI libgen li
entities.summary: Non-English document (de). Summary pending manual review.
entities.sourceRef: Pico Giovanni Giovanni Pico della Mirandola und die Entdeckung der jüdischen Mystik im italienischen Quattrocento GIULIO… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Pico's Heptaplus and Biblical Hermeneutics
entities.summary: This is a study in the history of allegory. It focuses on a particular theory of allegory, developed by the Italian philosopher Giovanni Pico della Mirandola in his Heptaplus (1489), a sevenfold commen- tary on the account of the six days of creation contained in Genesis 1.
entities.sourceRef: Pico's Heptaplus and Biblical Hermeneutics — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: PII: S0002-9610(37)91051-1
entities.summary: Cunha provides an early biographical sketch of Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa von Nettesheim for a medical audience, reflecting on the challenges of transmitting ancient manuscripts through monastic translation and the reliability of early modern printed texts. The piece, published in The American Journal of Surgery in 1937, treats Agrippa as a figure bridging medical and occult knowledge.

entities.name: Poisoning histories in the Italian renaissance: The case of Pico Della Mirandola and Angelo Poliziano
entities.summary: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola and Angelo Poliziano were two of the most important humanists of the Italian Reinassance. They died suddenly in 1494 and their deaths have been for centuries a subject of debate. The exhumation of their remains offered the opportunity to study the cause of their death through a multidisciplinary research project.
entities.sourceRef: Poisoning histories in the Italian renaissance: The case of Pico Della Mirandola and Angelo Poliziano — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Polygraphia and the Renaissance sign: The case of Trithemius
entities.summary: Glidden analyzes Trithemius's Polygraphiae libri sex (1518), the first printed manual of cryptography, as a point of confluence for medieval and Renaissance theories of language, arguing that Trithemius's encoding system stands between Scholastic scriptural exegesis and the seventeenth-century project of a universal philosophical language.
entities.sourceRef: Polygraphia and the Renaissance sign: The case of Trithemius — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: primus notes
entities.summary: Drewry and Drewry provide notes and translations for John Dee's Mysteriorum Liber Primus (1581-82), annotating Dee's Latin prayers, cabbalistic divine name formulas, and liturgical practices drawn from the Daily Office. The notes contextualize Dee's angel-conjuring sessions within both Christian devotional tradition and Reuchlin-Agrippa cabbalistic practice.

entities.name: primus notes
entities.summary: Drewry and Drewry provide detailed notes and English translations for the Mysteriorum Liber Primus, Dee's first book of angelic diaries (1581-1582), annotating the Latin prayers, liturgical sources, and cabbalistic practices that structured Dee's scrying sessions.

entities.name: primus notes
entities.summary: Drewry and Drewry provide detailed notes and English translations for the Mysteriorum Liber Primus, annotating the Latin prayers, liturgical structures, and cabbalistic naming practices that Dee employed in his angelic scrying sessions of 1581-1582. This appears to be a duplicate of document id 154.

entities.name: Princeps Concordiae: Pico della Mirandola and the Scholastic Tradition by Avery Dulles
entities.summary: Kibre reviews Dulles's Princeps Concordiae: Pico della Mirandola and the Scholastic Tradition, the first American monograph devoted primarily to Pico's philosophy, which aims to call attention to the medieval Scholastic foundations of Pico's syncretic philosophical system.
entities.sourceRef: Princeps Concordiae: Pico della Mirandola and the Scholastic Tradition by Avery Dulles — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Publications du Centre Européen d Etudes Bourguignonnes 1964 jan vol 6 Le Paige Frédéric Un humaniste des pays médians Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa de Nettesheim…
entities.summary: Le Paige surveys the literary afterlife of Cornelius Agrippa as a legendary magician in European literature, tracing his appearances in works by Shelley, Walter Scott, Rabelais, and Cyrano de Bergerac, and situating Agrippa as a humanist of the Burgundian pays medians.
entities.sourceRef: Publications du Centre Européen d Etudes Bourguignonnes 1964 jan vol 6 Le Paige Frédéric Un humaniste des pays médians H… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Pythagorean Number Symbolism, Alchemy, and the Disciplina Noua of John Dee's Monas Hieroglyphica
entities.summary: Tant dans sa Monas Hieroglyphica () que dans sa Mathematical Praeface (), John Dee défend une vue des mathématiques qui se veut compatible avec l’idée d’un ‘Pythago- ricall, and Platonicall perfect scholer’. Cet article montre que ce furent les doctrines arith- mologiques contenues dans les épîtres de Trithème—lues par Dee dans une copie du De vsu et mysteriis notarum (), qu’il acheta à Anvers en /—qui exercèrent sur les doctrines pythagoriciennes de la Monas de Dee un impact en...
entities.sourceRef: Pythagorean Number Symbolism, Alchemy, and the Disciplina Noua of John Dee's Monas Hieroglyphica — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Ramo n Lull Anthony Bonner Eve Bonner Doctor Illuminatus A Ramon Llull Reader
entities.summary: Bonner edits and translates a selection of Ramon Llull's works, including The Book of the Lover and the Beloved (newly translated by Eve Bonner), presenting Llull's combinatorial art, philosophical theology, and missionary program. The volume provides historical background, an account of Llull's thought, and a survey of the history of Lullism.
entities.sourceRef: Ramo n Lull Anthony Bonner Eve Bonner Doctor Illuminatus A Ramon Llull Reader — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Refiners Fire and the Yates Thesis Herme
entities.summary: Fleming, Asprem, and Taves reassess John Brooke's use of the Yates thesis in Refiner's Fire, arguing that the critique of Yates's notion of a discrete Hermetic tradition undermines the dichotomy between Christianity and hermeticism, and that situating Mormon sources within a richer history of Christianity better contextualizes Joseph Smith's radical ideas.
entities.sourceRef: Refiners Fire and the Yates Thesis Herme — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Renaissance Curiosa. John Dee's Conversation with Angels, Girolamo Cardano's Horoscope of Christ, Johannes Trithemius and Cryptography, George Dalgarno's Univer…
entities.summary: Obrist reviews Shumaker's Renaissance Curiosa, which treats John Dee's angelic conversations, Girolamo Cardano's horoscope of Christ, Johannes Trithemius's cryptography, and George Dalgarno's universal language as case studies in the psychology of ambitious Renaissance intellectuals.
entities.sourceRef: Renaissance Curiosa. John Dee's Conversation with Angels, Girolamo Cardano's Horoscope of Christ, Johannes Trithemius an… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Renaissance Magic and the Return of the Golden Age: The Occult Tradition and Marlowe, Jonson, and Shakespeare by John S. Mebane
entities.summary: Knoespel reviews John S. Mebane's Renaissance Magic and the Return of the Golden Age (1989), noting that the book surveys occultism's role in English Renaissance drama through chapters on Ficino, Pico, and Agrippa before applying this context to Marlowe, Jonson, and Shakespeare. Knoespel observes that Mebane situates his project within the Warburg tradition of Yates, Walker, and Shumaker but orients it toward traditional literary scholarship rather than history of science.
entities.sourceRef: Renaissance Magic and the Return of the Golden Age: The Occult Tradition and Marlowe, Jonson, and Shakespeare by John S.… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Renaissance Magic and the Return of the Golden Age: Utopianism and Religious Enthusiasm in "The Alchemist"
entities.summary: Mebane argues that Ben Jonson's The Alchemist constitutes a deliberate satire on Renaissance occultism's utopian and millenarian dimensions, demonstrating that Jonson recognized the connections between magical philosophy, political reform movements, and the heterodox doctrine of private divine inspiration or 'enthusiasm.'
entities.sourceRef: Renaissance Magic and the Return of the Golden Age: Utopianism and Religious Enthusiasm in "The Alchemist" — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Renaissance Philosophy. by Brian P. Copenhaver; Charles B. Schmitt
entities.summary: Wallace reviews Copenhaver and Schmitt's Renaissance Philosophy, appearing in the Sixteenth Century Journal. The review text extracted is largely occupied with a preceding review of a Shakespearean scholar, with only the bibliographic header of the Copenhaver-Schmitt review visible.
entities.sourceRef: Renaissance Philosophy. by Brian P. Copenhaver; Charles B. Schmitt — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Renaissance Quarterly vol 50 iss 4 Review by Leen Spruit Metaphysik und Mathemathik bei Giordano Bruno by Angelika Bönker Vallon 10 2307 3039457 libgen li
entities.summary: Spruit reviews Bonker-Vallon's monograph on metaphysics and mathematics in Giordano Bruno, which argues that Bruno's mathematical atomism has been misunderstood, that his doctrine of God plays a crucial but neglected role in his epistemology, and that his historical position between ancient Neoplatonism and early modern thought has been insufficiently appreciated.
entities.sourceRef: Renaissance Quarterly vol 50 iss 4 Review by Leen Spruit Metaphysik und Mathemathik bei Giordano Bruno by Angelika Bönke… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Renaissance Quarterly vol 53 iss 3 Rabin Sheila J Grell Ole Peter Brann Noel L Kieckhefer Paracelsus The Man and His Reputation His Ideas and Their Transformati…
entities.summary: This volume offers a postmodern meditation on Renaissance representations of nothing, positing the "zero-coin-counter-cipher-matrix-magician's circle-O" (24) as a key to Renaissance culture. The introduction looks at Titian's 1545 Danai- the cover illustration and frontispiece - which "functions as a parable of representation .
entities.sourceRef: Renaissance Quarterly vol 53 iss 3 Rabin Sheila J Grell Ole Peter Brann Noel L Kieckhefer Paracelsus The Man and His Rep… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Renaissance Quarterly vol 55 iss 1 Roush Sherry Dante as Piagnone Prophet Girolamo Benivieni s Cantico in laude di Dante 10 2307 1512532 libgen li
entities.summary: This study argues that Benivieni articulates his controversial, ideological vision in a necessarily prudent way by appropriating Dante as a safe and authoritative cultural icon and adopting a deliberately ambiguous symbolic language, which lauds himse4Fand hispolitics as much as it does Dante.
entities.sourceRef: Renaissance Quarterly vol 55 iss 1 Roush Sherry Dante as Piagnone Prophet Girolamo Benivieni s Cantico in laude di Dante… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Renaissance Quarterly vol 61 iss 3 Review by Marion Leathers Kuntz M V Dougherty Pico della Mirandola New Essays 10 1353 ren 0 0161 libgen li
entities.summary: Kuntz reviews Dougherty's edited volume Pico della Mirandola: New Essays (Cambridge, 2008), praising its well-balanced collection of studies on Pico's rhetoric, theology, philosophy of religion, metaphysics, and biblical interpretation. The review highlights contributions by Kraye on rhetoric and philosophy, Blum on Pico and the Church, Sudduth on philosophy of religion, and Allen on Pico's Commento and Heptaplus.
entities.sourceRef: Renaissance Quarterly vol 61 iss 3 Review by Marion Leathers Kuntz M V Dougherty Pico della Mirandola New Essays 10 1353… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: renq69407 1413..1613
entities.summary: Copenhaver reviews Marrone's A History of Science, Magic and Belief: From Medieval to Early Modern Europe, praising its ambitious longue duree synthesis while noting that silence about Ficino's revival of ancient Neoplatonism distorts the record of Quattrocento and Cinquecento magic, since the most distinctive feature of that period's learned magic was its philosophical grounding in the Neoplatonic tradition.

entities.name: renq70206 633..829
entities.summary: Spruit reviews Paul Richard Blum's Giordano Bruno Teaches Aristotle (English translation, 2016), which argues that Bruno had precise knowledge of Aristotle's works and deliberately used ancient philosophers to develop his own original positions. Spruit notes Blum's Kantian reading, in which Bruno seeks a transcendental logic and the rational construction of scientific systems to approach extramental reality.

entities.name: renq70206 633..829
entities.summary: Spruit reviews Blum's Giordano Bruno Teaches Aristotle, assessing its argument that Bruno possessed precise knowledge of Aristotle's works and deliberately used the philosophical tradition to develop original positions, particularly regarding transcendental logic, cosmological principles, and the relationship between unity and multiplicity.

entities.name: renq70206 633..829
entities.summary: Copenhaver reviews Ciliberto's three-volume encyclopedic work Giordano Bruno: Parole, concetti, immagini, praising it as a definitive skeleton key to Bruno's often elusive terminology, his reception in Italian political culture, and the philosophical vocabulary that shaped his cosmological and metaphysical thought.

entities.name: Reuchlin and Erasmus: Humanism and Occult Philosophy1
entities.summary: Zika examines the fundamental intellectual divergence between Reuchlin and Erasmus beneath their shared humanist label, arguing that Reuchlin's engagement with Cabala and occult philosophy represented a style of religiosity fundamentally distinct from Erasmus's program, and that this distinction helps explain Erasmus's shifting and ultimately distancing attitude toward the Reuchlin controversy over Jewish books.
entities.sourceRef: Reuchlin and Erasmus: Humanism and Occult Philosophy1 — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: REUCHLIN UND PICO DELLA MIRANDOLA
entities.summary: Beierwaltes examines the philosophical relationship between Reuchlin and Pico della Mirandola within the Neoplatonic tradition, tracing how Reuchlin drew on Pico's synthesis of Kabbalah and Neoplatonic philosophy, and situating both figures within the broader context of Ficino's Platonic revival and Cusanus's speculative thought.
entities.sourceRef: REUCHLIN UND PICO DELLA MIRANDOLA — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Review
entities.summary: Russell reviews Hakansson's Seeing the Word: John Dee and Renaissance Occultism, which addresses the twofold scholarly problem of Dee's intellectual range and the apparent discontinuity between his mathematical and occult pursuits.

entities.name: Revue Bénédictine 1976 jan vol 86 iss 1 2 Howie David I Benedictine Monks Manuscripts Copying and the Renaissance Johannes Trithemius De laude scriptorum 10 148…
entities.summary: Howie analyzes Trithemius's De Laude Scriptorum (1492) as a document of Renaissance monastic humanism, demonstrating how Trithemius incorporated humanist values -- historicism, educational ideals, and the vita activa -- into his program for Benedictine reform and his defense of manuscript copying in the age of print.
entities.sourceRef: Revue Bénédictine 1976 jan vol 86 iss 1 2 Howie David I Benedictine Monks Manuscripts Copying and the Renaissance Johann… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: RQX_73_4_BookReviews 1328..1330
entities.summary: Marsh reviews Borghesi's critical edition of Giovanni Pico della Mirandola's Lettere, a collection of seventy-four letters based on two principal witnesses (Vatican codex Capponi 235 and the 1496 editio princeps), noting the modest dimensions of the surviving correspondence and the long gestation of the editorial project dating back to 1939.

entities.name: rrsq337713 433..456
entities.summary: Miles examines the paradox of magical language in Cornelius Agrippa's works, problematizing the assumption that magical discourse simply identifies signifier with signified and exploring how Agrippa's rhetorical strategies complicate the relationship between occult philosophy, theology, and empirical approaches to language.

entities.name: Sacralizing the Secular: The Renaissance Origins of Modernity (review)
entities.summary: Copenhaver reviews McKnight's Sacralizing the Secular, which argues that Renaissance recovery of ancient wisdom traditions produced a concept of sacralization complementary to secularization, criticizing the work for excessive abstraction and imprecise handling of the intellectual history of hermeticism, Gnosticism, and Renaissance Neoplatonism.
entities.sourceRef: Sacralizing the Secular: The Renaissance Origins of Modernity (review) — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: SavPicoPresentRSA2012
entities.summary: Walden examines the intellectual affinities between Savonarola and Pico della Mirandola, arguing that both figures have suffered interpretive distortion -- Pico's Oration read in a wholly secular fashion, Savonarola's political extremism eclipsing his theological foundations -- and that their long friendship rested on compatible religious and philosophical commitments.

entities.name: Scholastic Florence: Moral Psychology in the Quattrocento
entities.summary: Edelheit examines the intersection of Scholastic philosophy and humanist culture in Quattrocento Florence, focusing on moral psychology as treated by university-trained scholars, Dominican reformers, and humanist intellectuals, with attention to the continuity of Scholastic traditions within the supposedly anti-Scholastic Florentine Renaissance milieu.
entities.sourceRef: Scholastic Florence: Moral Psychology in the Quattrocento — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Schriften zur Rechtsgeschichte 77 Ackermann Markus Rafael Der Jurist Johannes Reuchlin 10 3790 978 3 428 49793 5 libgen li
entities.summary: Ackermann examines Johannes Reuchlin's career as a jurist, reconstructing from archival sources his legal education, his service as a learned councillor, and his role as judge of the Swabian League, situating Reuchlin's humanist and Hebraist activities within the context of his primary professional identity as a doctor of law.
entities.sourceRef: Schriften zur Rechtsgeschichte 77 Ackermann Markus Rafael Der Jurist Johannes Reuchlin 10 3790 978 3 428 49793 5 libgen… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: SDA
entities.summary: This is a recreation of the Sigillum Dei Aemeth diagram from Dee's manuscript (Sloane 3188), presenting the complex heptagonal and heptagrammatic seal with its angelic names (Filiæ Bonitatis, Filii Lucis, etc.) and planetary attributions, as reconstructed by Clay Holden for the John Dee Publication Project.

entities.name: Self-Knowledge Illumination and Natural notes on Pico sources
entities.summary: Pirtea examines the ancient sources of Pico della Mirandola's esotericism, focusing on the intersection of self-knowledge, divine illumination, and natural magic in Pico's thought, and tracing continuities between late antique esoteric traditions and their Renaissance reception.
entities.sourceRef: Self-Knowledge Illumination and Natural notes on Pico sources — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Simon Young Helen Killick An Analysis of Keith Thomas s Religion and the Decline of Magic
entities.summary: Young and Killick provide a Macat-style critical analysis of Keith Thomas's Religion and the Decline of Magic, contextualizing its arguments and significance for students and scholars. The volume serves as an educational guide to Thomas's landmark study of popular beliefs in sixteenth- and seventeenth-century England.
entities.sourceRef: Simon Young Helen Killick An Analysis of Keith Thomas s Religion and the Decline of Magic — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.summary: Rattansi reviews Josten's translation and annotation of Dee's Monas Hieroglyphica, noting that the work reveals the numerological, cabbalistic, and astrological speculations intertwined with Renaissance alchemy, while acknowledging the baffling character of much of its alchemical import.
entities.sourceRef: Sixteenth and Seventeenth Centuries A Translation of John Dee's “Monas Hieroglyphica” (Antwerp, 1564), with an Introduct… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

Mehl notes the addition of Maurer's essay on Reuchlin and Judaism, though he observes this 1952 piece needs supplementation by more recent work.

entities.name: SOLVED: THE CIPHERS IN BOOK III OF TRITHEMIUS'S STEGANOGRAPHIA
entities.summary: Reeds demonstrates that the ciphers in Book III of Trithemius's Steganographia, long thought to contain genuine demonic conjurations, are in fact polyalphabetic ciphers encoding intelligible plaintext messages. Published in Cryptologia in 1998, the article resolves a centuries-old debate about whether the Steganographia's third book was truly about cryptography or about angel magic.
entities.sourceRef: SOLVED: THE CIPHERS IN BOOK III OF TRITHEMIUS'S STEGANOGRAPHIA — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: test
entities.summary: This is the Marquette University Press edition of Pico della Mirandola's De Ente et Uno (Of Being and Unity), translated by Victor Michael Hamm, the only finished treatise of Pico's projected Symphonia Platonis et Aristotelis, in which he argues for the fundamental agreement between Plato and Aristotle on the relationship between being and the One.

entities.summary: Kibre reconstructs the contents and significance of Pico della Mirandola's privately owned library, one of the most celebrated of the fifteenth century. Published by Columbia University Press in 1936, the study examines the literary resources available to a scholar whose short but brilliant career made a profound impression on his contemporaries.
entities.sourceRef: The American Historical Review 1937 oct The Library of Pico della Mirandola By Pearl Kibre 10 1086 ahr 43 1 109 libgen l… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Ash Wednesday Supper
entities.summary: Gatti presents a new English translation with facing Italian text of Bruno's La Cena de le Ceneri (1584), the first of his six Italian dialogues composed in London, in which Bruno lays out a revolutionary infinite cosmology extending the Copernican model and recounts his clashes with conservative academics at Oxford and London.
entities.sourceRef: The Ash Wednesday Supper — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Beginning of the World in Renaissance Jewish Thought
entities.summary: Ogren examines how Italian Jewish philosophers and kabbalists between 1492 and 1535 interpreted the biblical creation narrative (Ma'aseh Bereshit), focusing on Jochanan Allemanno, Isaac Abravanel, and Leone Ebreo in relation to Giovanni Pico della Mirandola's synthesis of philosophy and Kabbalah.
entities.sourceRef: The Beginning of the World in Renaissance Jewish Thought — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Beginning of the World in Renaissance Jewish Thought
entities.summary: Ogren examines Italian Jewish philosophical and Kabbalistic interpretations of ma'aseh bereshit (the work of creation) from 1492 to 1535, focusing on Johanan Allemanno, Isaac Abravanel, and Leone Ebreo, and their intellectual interactions with Pico della Mirandola's syncretist project.
entities.sourceRef: The Beginning of the World in Renaissance Jewish Thought — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Cabala of Pegasus
entities.summary: Sondergard and Sowell translate and annotate Giordano Bruno's Cabala del cavallo pegaseo (1585), presenting the text for both general readers and cultural historians of the early modern period. The translators note Bruno's status as a figure variously identified as Galileo's precursor, a martyr for modern science, a Hermetic magus, and a kabbalist mystic.
entities.sourceRef: The Cabala of Pegasus — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Calls of Enoch: The First Key of Enochian Magic

entities.summary: Strauss reviews Brann's The Abbot Trithemius (1462-1516): The Renaissance of Monastic Humanism, a study devoted to Trithemius's claim to historical importance as a figure who combined monastic reform with humanist intellectual ambitions in late fifteenth-century Germany.
entities.sourceRef: The Catholic Historical Review 1983 oct vol 69 iss 4 Review by Gerald Strauss The Abbot Trithemius The Renaissance of Mo… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Classical Weekly vol 38 iss 1 Sheehan Daniel F Hamm Victor Michael Pico della Mirandola of Being and Unity 10 2307 4341978 libgen li
entities.summary: Sheehan reviews Hamm's translation of Pico della Mirandola's De Ente et Uno (Of Being and Unity), assessing the translation's contribution to making Pico's ontological treatise accessible to English-language readers.
entities.sourceRef: The Classical Weekly vol 38 iss 1 Sheehan Daniel F Hamm Victor Michael Pico della Mirandola of Being and Unity 10 2307 4… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The English Historical Review 1994 feb vol 109 iss 430 Review by Brian P Copenhaver The Origins of the Platonic Academy of Florenceby Arthur Field 10 2307 57492…
entities.summary: Copenhaver reviews Arthur Field's The Origins of the Platonic Academy of Florence (1990), praising it as a persuasive account of philosophical learning in quattrocento Florence that removes the city's intellectual achievements from the historical vacuum in which they have often been viewed. Copenhaver notes that Field enriches understanding of both Platonic and Aristotelian philosophy in relation to Florentine politics and society.
entities.sourceRef: The English Historical Review 1994 feb vol 109 iss 430 Review by Brian P Copenhaver The Origins of the Platonic Academy… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The European Legacy 2006 dec vol 11 iss 7 Ferraro Bruno Giordano Bruno s Infinitely Numerous Worlds and Lunar Literature 10 1080 10848770601023040 libgen li
entities.summary: This study explores some of the scientific and philosophical issues arising from Giordano Bruno’s (1548–1600) writings and attempts to point out the possible sources for his cosmological views and how they may be regarded as a point of reference for later philosophers; furthermore, it highlights how Bruno’s cosmological intuitions may have been incorporated in later literary works of an imaginative and astronomical-speculative nature.
entities.sourceRef: The European Legacy 2006 dec vol 11 iss 7 Ferraro Bruno Giordano Bruno s Infinitely Numerous Worlds and Lunar Literature… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Hieroglyphic Monad
entities.summary: Dee presents his Monas Hieroglyphica (1564), a treatise dedicated to Emperor Maximilian that unfolds through a series of theorems the symbolic properties of a composite hieroglyphic sign incorporating the Sun, Moon, cross, and zodiacal elements, claiming to demonstrate through geometry and number the first principles underlying all natural and celestial phenomena.

entities.name: The Hieroglyphic Monad
entities.summary: Dee presents his Monas Hieroglyphica (1564), a treatise dedicated to Emperor Maximilian unfolding through numbered theorems the symbolic geometry of a composite hieroglyphic sign representing the unity underlying celestial and terrestrial phenomena. This appears to be a duplicate of document 157 in the corpus.

entities.name: The Influence of Marsilio Ficino (1433-1494) on Elizabethan Literature: Christopher Marlowe and William Shakespeare
entities.summary: These two volumes are the first or opening section of an extensive study of the influence of Marsilio Ficino on major English poets. Ficino lived in Florence, Italy from 1433 to 1499. He introduced Plato to the Renaissance by his translations of the philosopher's complete works with detailed commentary. Prior to Ficino, the only Platonic dialogue available was the Tin2aeus.
entities.sourceRef: The Influence of Marsilio Ficino (1433-1494) on Elizabethan Literature: Christopher Marlowe and William Shakespeare — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.summary: Non-English document (he). Summary pending manual review.

entities.name: The Language of Demons and Angels and Cornelius Agrippas Occult Philosophy
entities.summary: Lehrich offers a semiotic reading of Cornelius Agrippa's De occulta philosophia, arguing that the work's coherence lies in its theory of language and signification rather than in any systematic natural philosophy. Published in Brill's Studies in Intellectual History, the monograph treats Agrippa's magic as fundamentally concerned with the relationship between demonic and angelic language and the structure of occult meaning.
entities.sourceRef: The Language of Demons and Angels and Cornelius Agrippas Occult Philosophy — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Magician, the Witch, and the Law (review)
entities.summary: Copenhaver reviews Edward Peters's The Magician, the Witch, and the Law, engaging with Peters's argument that medieval conceptions of learned magic furnished an important background, alongside heresy, for later notions of witchcraft.
entities.sourceRef: The Magician, the Witch, and the Law (review) — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Magician, the Witch, and the Law (review)
entities.summary: Copenhaver reviews Edward Peters's The Magician, the Witch, and the Law, engaging with Peters's analysis of the legal and intellectual categories through which medieval and early modern authorities distinguished licit from illicit magical practice.
entities.sourceRef: The Magician, the Witch, and the Law (review) — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Monas Hieroglyphica and the Alchemical Thread of John Dee's Career
entities.summary: Clulee traces the alchemical thread running through John Dee's entire career, arguing that the Monas Hieroglyphica (1564) represents Dee's most enduring achievement: an inventive symbolic language integrating alchemy with his conviction of the mathematical nature of divine creation, uniting the principles of alchemy and astronomy within a geometrical cabbala.
entities.sourceRef: The Monas Hieroglyphica and the Alchemical Thread of John Dee's Career — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Preservation of Jewish Religious Books in Sixteenth-Century Germany: Johannes Reuchlin's Augenspiegel
entities.summary: Johannes Reuchlin’s Augenspiegel is a text of scholarship and sophistication that has attracted comment and analysis since it was first published in 1511. This derives from its aim which was to prevent the destruction of Jewish religious texts, a form of literature considered at best irrelevant or at worst a threat to the Christian culture of sixteenth-century Europe.
entities.sourceRef: The Preservation of Jewish Religious Books in Sixteenth-Century Germany: Johannes Reuchlin's Augenspiegel — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Proto-Protestant Assault upon Church Magic: The ‘Errores Bohemanorum‘ According to the Abbot Trithemius (1462-1516)
entities.summary: Brann examines Trithemius's attack on the Hussite 'errores Bohemanoum' as evidence for a proto-Protestant assault on church-sanctioned magic predating the Reformation proper. Brann argues that Trithemius's dual position as a practicing magician and defender of ecclesiastical orthodoxy illuminates the strained distinction between learned magic and demonic sorcery in late medieval thought, extending Keith Thomas's thesis about magic's critics beyond English Lollard sources to the continent.
entities.sourceRef: The Proto-Protestant Assault upon Church Magic: The ‘Errores Bohemanorum‘ According to the Abbot Trithemius (1462-1516) — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Proto-Protestant Assault upon Church Magic: The ‘Errores Bohemanorum‘ According to the Abbot Trithemius (1462-1516)
entities.summary: Brann examines Trithemius's attack on the 'Bohemian errors' of the Hussites as a case study in the proto-Protestant assault upon church magic, arguing that Trithemius's dual identity as both a practicing magician and a defender of Catholic sacramental power reveals the strained distinction between legitimate ecclesiastical rites and illicit magical operations that the Reformation would ultimately exploit.
entities.sourceRef: The Proto-Protestant Assault upon Church Magic: The ‘Errores Bohemanorum‘ According to the Abbot Trithemius (1462-1516) — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Royal Typographer and the Alchemist: John Dee, Willem Silvius, and the Diagrammatic Alchemy of the Monas Hieroglyphica
entities.summary: This article considers the reasons why Dee chose to collaborate with Silvius, and the importance of the intellectual culture – and the print trade – of the Low Countries to the development of Dee’s outlook. Dee’s Monas was probably the first alchemical work which focused exclusively on the diagrammatic representation of the alchemical process, combining diagrams, cosmological schemes, and various forms of tabular grid.
entities.sourceRef: The Royal Typographer and the Alchemist: John Dee, Willem Silvius, and the Diagrammatic Alchemy of the Monas Hieroglyphi… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The South Central Bulletin vol 38 iss 1 Review by Dorothy H Brown Pico della Mirandola s Heptaplus or Discourse on the Seven Days of Creation by Jessie Brewer M…
entities.summary: Brown reviews McGaw's translation of Pico della Mirandola's Heptaplus, or Discourse on the Seven Days of Creation, Pico's sevenfold allegorical interpretation of Genesis drawing on Neoplatonic, Kabbalistic, and Christian sources.
entities.sourceRef: The South Central Bulletin vol 38 iss 1 Review by Dorothy H Brown Pico della Mirandola s Heptaplus or Discourse on the S… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: The Thomist A Speculative Quarterly Review 2014 jul 1 vol 78 iss 3 Salas Victor M Giovanni Pico Della Mirandola on Being and Unity A Thomistic Solution to an An…
entities.summary: Salas argues that Pico della Mirandola's De ente et uno employs Thomas Aquinas's real distinction between esse and essentia to reconcile Neoplatonic and Aristotelian metaphysics through the notion of participation. Salas situates Pico's argument as a two-pronged attempt to establish metaphysical accord between Plato and Aristotle, consistent with his reputation as the Princeps Concordiae.
entities.sourceRef: The Thomist A Speculative Quarterly Review 2014 jul 1 vol 78 iss 3 Salas Victor M Giovanni Pico Della Mirandola on Being… — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)

entities.name: Thinking with Demons: The Idea of Witchcraft in Early Modern Europe
entities.summary: Clark undertakes a comprehensive intellectual history of witchcraft beliefs in early modern Europe, arguing that demonological writings must be understood as coherent contributions to contemporary debates in natural philosophy, theology, and political theory rather than dismissed as irrational superstition.
entities.sourceRef: Thinking with Demons: The Idea of Witchcraft in Early Modern Europe — Renaissance Magic (RMDB)
